text: Today we present a case of superior mesenteric artery syndrome four months following sleeve gastrectomy. There are no disclosures. The patient is a thirty-nine-year-old female who underwent laparoscopic sleeve gastrectomy for a BMI of thirty-six. She initially did well with rapid weight loss, then developed unrelenting nausea, emesis, and epigastric pain. At the time of consultation four months post-operatively, she had lost seventy pounds to a BMI of twenty-four.

CT scan demonstrated significant narrowing of the duodenum between the aorta and SMA with dilation of the duodenum proximal to this, suspicious for SMA syndrome. Duplex sonography demonstrated a decreased angle between the aorta and SMA to eighteen degrees and a distance of five point eight millimeters, diagnostic for SMA syndrome.

SMA syndrome is obstruction of the duodenum due to compression between the aorta and SMA. This is often precipitated by extreme weight loss. Symptoms are nonspecific but have been well described in the literature with postprandial fullness, nausea, epigastric pain, and emesis. Symptoms improve with sitting forward or in the left lateral decubitus position.

The diagnostic criteria by duplex or CT require an aortomesenteric angle less than twenty-five and distance less than ten millimeters, both of which were present in this patient. SMA syndrome is extremely rare, occurring in less than one percent of the general population. In the bariatric population, the incidence is based only on case reports.

Surgical options include gastrojejunostomy, but Strong's procedure and duodenal jejunostomy are best described. Strong's procedure involves division of the ligament of trites with duodenal mobilization and caudal displacement of the duodenal jejunal flexure. Duodenal jejunostomy is also well described and has a success rate of seventy-nine to one hundred percent. The first laparoscopic case was described in nineteen ninety-eight.

During the laparoscopic duodenal jejunostomy, endoscopy reveals the gastric sleeve of normal caliber. In the third portion of the duodenum, visible pulsations of the aorta and SMA are noted, with significant narrowing of the duodenum. The surgeon reflects the transverse colon to identify the middle colic vessels, and the duodenum, middle colic vessels, and SMA are clearly identified.

The surgeon uses laparoscopy and endoscopy to confirm the duodenal narrowing at the level where the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) crosses the duodenum. The chosen site for anastomosis is just proximal to this narrowing.

The surgeon incises the peritoneal lining of the duodenum and mobilizes the duodenum distally and then proximally. A limb of jejunum, approximately thirty centimeters distal to the ligament of Treitz, is prepared for anastomosis. A stay suture is placed to orient the anastomosis.

The surgeon creates enterotomies in the duodenum and jejunum, using a linear stapler to form the anastomosis while carefully avoiding damage to the superior mesenteric artery at the distal tip of the stapler.
The surgeon places a stay suture posteriorly to orient the common aneurotomy. The defect in the anastomosis is then over-sown in two layers using a running vicryl suture.

The surgeon repeats endoscopy to inspect the anastomosis intraluminally. Methylene blue is instilled through the endoscope to perform a leak test, checking for any signs of leakage at the anastomosis site.

The patient did well postoperatively, was started on a liquid diet on post-op day two, and discharged home on post-op day five. At follow-up several months later, she noted dramatic improvement in her symptoms.

SMA syndrome is a rare diagnosis, but should be considered after extreme weight loss, especially in the bariatric population. Laparoscopic duodenal jejunostomy is a technically easy and successful option for the management of SMA syndrome.
